1: The triple summons of priests, Israel, and royal house to hear YHWH's judgment is the OT prelude to the scene in which Christ stands before the same three structures -- priesthood (Caiaphas), people (the crowd), and royal authority (Pilate) -- and is condemned by all of them. The judgment that Hosea announces falls on those who were meant to administer it; at the trial of Jesus, the administrators of judgment become its perpetrators. But in condemning the one who was innocent, they enact the judgment of the covenant lawsuit on the one who bore it in their place (Acts 2:23: 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men').
2: The rebels sinking deep in their corruption while YHWH has been a discipline to all of them captures the human tendency to deepen transgression even as divine correction multiplies. Christ came into the full depth of this corruption: 'He had to be made like his brothers in every respect, so that he might become a merciful and faithful high priest... For because he himself has suffered when tempted, he is able to help those who are being tempted' (Hebrews 2:17-18). He entered the deepest pit of human failure not as one who stood above it but as one who was made like those in it -- so that he could pull them out from within.
3: YHWH's comprehensive knowledge of Ephraim ('I know Ephraim') is the backdrop for Christ's all-knowing intimacy with his people: 'I am the good shepherd. I know my own and my own know me' (John 10:14). The defilement of Israel that YHWH sees clearly -- nothing hidden from him -- is equally visible to Christ: 'he knew all people and needed no one to bear witness about man, for he himself knew what was in man' (John 2:24-25). But the knowing is not punitive judgment only; the shepherd knows the sheep in order to protect, guide, and lay down his life for them (John 10:11).
4: The deeds that prevent Israel from returning to God -- and the spirit of prostitution within them -- describe the bondage of the will that cannot repent by its own effort. Christ addresses this at the root: 'No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him' (John 6:44). The new birth is required because the spirit of prostitution renders self-generated return impossible: 'That which is born of the flesh is flesh, and that which is born of the Spirit is spirit' (John 3:6). The Spirit of God must overcome the spirit of prostitution, replacing the inward drive away from God with the inward drive toward him -- the new heart of Ezekiel 36:26-27, which Christ gives.
5: Israel's arrogance testifying against him to his face is the self-condemnation of pride, contrasted with Christ's humility which stands in complete contrast: 'Though he was in the form of God, he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant' (Philippians 2:6-7). The pride that made Ephraim stumble is the opposite of the kenosis that made Christ the source of salvation. The stumbling of arrogance is answered by the exaltation of humility: 'God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble' (James 4:6) -- the principle Hosea illustrates is the principle Christ embodies.
6: Seeking YHWH with flocks and herds but not finding him because he has withdrawn is the failure of ritual without covenant. Christ is the one through whom YHWH is truly found: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6). The seeking with sacrificial animals (religious effort) that produces no access to God is answered by the Lamb who himself is the access: 'through him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father' (Ephesians 2:18). The sacrifices that Israel brought to find YHWH are replaced by the one sacrifice through which YHWH is permanently accessible.
7: The unfaithfulness to YHWH in bearing illegitimate children -- the syncretistic offspring of mixed worship -- is answered in Christ by the new birth that creates truly legitimate children of God: 'to all who did receive him, who believed in his name, he gave the right to become children of God, who were born, not of blood nor of the will of the flesh nor of the will of man, but of God' (John 1:12-13). The legitimacy problem of Hosea's Israel (children of zenutim) is resolved by adoption through Christ into the family of God -- a legitimacy that no syncretic compromise can take away.
8: The ram's horn and trumpet sounding the war alarm at Gibeah and Ramah are the OT's instruments of crisis. Christ reframes the same instruments in eschatological context: the trumpet that sounds at the end of the age (1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16; Matthew 24:31) announces not invasion but resurrection and gathering. The alarm-trumpet of Hosea's judgment becomes the gathering-trumpet of Christ's parousia. The crisis announced by Hosea's horn is absorbed in the cross; the trumpet of the last day announces the completion of what the cross secured.
9: Ephraim's desolation on the day of punishment is the covenant curse fully enacted. Christ absorbs this curse for those who are in him: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). The certain (nemannah, trustworthy) declaration of YHWH about the judgment becomes, in Christ, the equally certain declaration of life: 'Truly, truly, I say to you, whoever hears my word and believes him who sent me has eternal life. He does not come into judgment, but has passed from death to life' (John 5:24).
10: The princes of Judah who move boundary stones -- defrauding the weak and expanding at others' expense -- are the antitype of Christ who voluntarily gave up what was his: 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich' (2 Corinthians 8:9). Where Judah's leaders seized others' territory, Christ surrendered his own. The wrath poured like water on those who defraud is met by the grace poured out on those who are in Christ: 'he poured out on us richly through Jesus Christ our Savior' (Titus 3:6).
11: Ephraim crushed and oppressed in justice, determined to pursue worthless idols, is the picture of the people Paul describes as enslaved to the elemental powers: 'formerly, when you did not know God, you were enslaved to those that by nature are not gods' (Galatians 4:8). Christ's liberation from the crushing power of idolatry is the NT gospel: 'For freedom Christ has set us free; stand firm therefore, and do not submit again to a yoke of slavery' (Galatians 5:1). The crushing of Ephraim that worthless idol-pursuit produces is reversed by the freedom that knowing the true God in Christ provides.
12: YHWH as moth to Ephraim and rot to Judah -- the slow invisible judgment -- contrasts with Christ who is the light that makes hidden things visible: 'For nothing is hidden that will not be made manifest, nor is anything secret that will not be known' (Luke 8:17). The slow decay of Hosea's moth-judgment is brought to its end in Christ's cross: the full accounting of sin is made visible there, where nothing is hidden. The rot that was destroying Israel from within is fully exposed and dealt with on the cross -- not ignored but exhausted in the one who bears it.
13: Ephraim seeking Assyria for healing -- the political solution to a covenant problem -- is the paradigm of all misdirected help-seeking. Christ names himself as the physician who addresses what no political or human power can cure: 'Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick. I came not to call the righteous, but sinners' (Matthew 9:12-13). The great king of Assyria cannot heal the covenant wound; the Great Physician can. The seeking of foreign kings for help that only YHWH can give is the structural sin that the incarnation corrects by making the divine healer personally, humanly accessible.
14: YHWH as lion tearing and carrying away -- divine judgment from which no one can rescue -- is the fearsome backdrop against which Christ's rescue mission is defined. He came specifically to rescue from the lion: 'Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour' (1 Peter 5:8) -- and Christ is the stronger one who overcomes the strong man (Matthew 12:29; Luke 11:21-22). The divine lion of judgment (Christ is also the Lion of Judah, Revelation 5:5) is simultaneously the Lamb who was slain -- the one who absorbed the divine judgment so that the judgment-lion would not devour his people.
15: YHWH's return to his place -- withdrawing from Israel until they acknowledge their guilt and seek his face -- foreshadows Christ's ascension and the inter-advent waiting. Christ returns to the Father's right hand (Acts 1:9-11; Hebrews 9:24) and 'those who eagerly await him' will see him return 'a second time... to save those who are eagerly waiting for him' (Hebrews 9:28). The distress that drives Israel to seek YHWH earnestly is the same dynamic the Spirit uses in the inter-advent period: the consciousness of need drives the earnest seeking that the returning Lord rewards. 'In their distress they will earnestly seek me' -- Christ's parousia answers this earnest seeking at the last.
